core_ideas: 1. The more kinetic energy an object has the more damage it can do in a collision.
The more kinetic energy an object has the more you have to push against the direction of its motion to get it to stop.
The kinetic energy of an object is directly proportional to its mass; the KE of an object is proportional to the square of its speed.
misconceptions: a) Kinetic energy depends on mass only. (Liu & Fang, 2017)
b) Double of speed double kinetic energy. (Liu & Fang, 2017)
c) Kinetic energy is momentum. (Liu & Fang, 2017; Dalaklioglu et al.,
d) An object has energy within it that is used up as the object moves.

quiz_items.stem: Car A and Car B have the same mass. Car B is moving twice as fast as Car A. How does the kinetic energy of Car B compare to Car A?
How confident are you about your answer?
Cart A has mass 2 kg and moves at 2 m/s. Cart B has mass 1 kg and moves at 4 m/s. Which cart has more kinetic energy?

options.A: Cart A, because it has more mass.
options.B: Cart B, because it is moving faster.
options.C: They have the same kinetic energy because one has double the mass and the other has double the speed.
options.D: You can't tell without knowing how far each cart travels.

quiz_items.matched_misconception: A for a), C for b)

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
Two carts move toward identical foam barriers. Both carts have the same momentum, but Cart A is heavier and moving slower, while Cart B is lighter and moving faster. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: They have the same kinetic energy because their momentum is the same.
options.B: Cart B has more kinetic energy because higher speed increases kinetic energy more than mass does.
options.C: Cart A has more kinetic energy because it has more mass.
options.D: You cannot compare kinetic energy unless you know the stopping time.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
A puck glides across very smooth ice at a nearly constant speed for several seconds. Which statement best describes what happens to the puck's kinetic energy while it keeps moving at the same speed?

options.A: It decreases because the puck uses up its kinetic energy to keep moving.
options.B: It stays about the same because its speed stays about the same.
options.C: It increases because moving objects build up kinetic energy over time.
options.D: It becomes momentum, so kinetic energy is gone.

quiz_items.matched_misconception: A for d), D for c)

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
